query.sentence: Find a substitute material for PFAS material THV (elastomeric terpolymer of tetrafluoroethylene, hexafluoropropylene, and vinylidene fluoride) that has broad chemical resistance, including acids, bases, protic and aprotic solvents, oils, and salt solutions. Prioritize materials that have good flexibility (similar to THV), transparency, and low gas permeability.
query.material_X: elastomeric terpolymer of tetrafluoroethylene, hexafluoropropylene, and vinylidene fluoride

properties: Broad-spectrum chemical resistance to strong mineral acids, dilute and moderate bases, aqueous salt solutions, aliphatic hydrocarbons, lubricating oils, alcohols, and many polar aprotic solvents
Retention of mechanical integrity after prolonged liquid exposure with low swelling and low extractables
High flexibility/elastomer-like compliance relative to rigid engineering thermoplastics; low modulus and good bendability at room temperature
Optical transparency or at least high translucency in practical thicknesses
Low gas permeability relative to commodity elastomers and polyolefins
Good thermal stability for typical chemical-service temperatures, preferably continuous use above 100 C
Processability into films, tubing, liners, seals, or molded parts at laboratory scale
constraints: Candidate must be completely PFAS-free and contain no carbon-fluorine bonds
Must substitute for THV in chemically aggressive service where fluoropolymer-like resistance is desired
Should prioritize flexibility similar to THV over maximum stiffness
Should prioritize transparency over opaque high-performance polymers
Should avoid materials known to be strongly attacked or heavily swollen by common aprotic solvents such as ketones, chlorinated solvents, NMP, DMF, or THF if broad chemical resistance is claimed
Should have lower gas permeability than silicone and typical thermoplastic elastomers if possible
Must be realistically manufacturable at lab scale using commercially accessible polymer processing methods

final_candidate.material_name: Plasticized polyvinyl chloride (PVC-P), medical/chemical-grade transparent flexible PVC formulation
final_candidate.justification: Among PFAS-free transparent flexible materials, no single polymer fully matches THV across acids, bases, protic and aprotic solvents, oils, and low gas permeability. Flexible PVC is the best practical compromise when the priorities are broad chemical resistance, flexibility, transparency, and relatively low gas permeability. Compared with TPU, EVA, SEBS, and silicone, flexible PVC generally offers better resistance to aqueous acids, bases, salts, many oils, and many alcohols, while also providing substantially lower gas permeability than silicone and many soft elastomers. It is inherently transparent in properly compounded form and can be tuned over a wide hardness range to approach THV-like flexibility. PVC also has better barrier performance than most elastomers because of its polar, relatively dense chain structure. The main limitation is that PVC is not universally resistant to all aprotic solvents: ketones, cyclic ethers, chlorinated solvents, and some strong polar aprotic solvents can cause swelling or attack, and plasticizer extraction can occur depending on formulation. Therefore it is not a perfect one-for-one fluoropolymer replacement, but within the PFAS-free design space it is the most balanced transparent flexible barrier material that is commercially established and lab-processable. To maximize chemical robustness, the formulation should use a high-molecular-weight polymeric plasticizer or trimellitate plasticizer, low extractables stabilizer package, and minimal additives.

process_recipe.description: Select a suspension-grade PVC resin suitable for flexible transparent compounds and define target hardness/barrier tradeoff
process_recipe.conditions: K-value approximately 65-70; target Shore A about 75-90 depending on flexibility requirement

process_recipe.description: Prepare transparent flexible formulation using low-volatility, low-extractable plasticizer and heat stabilizer package
process_recipe.conditions: Typical lab starting formulation per 100 phr PVC: 25-45 phr polymeric polyester plasticizer or TOTM, 2-5 phr epoxidized soybean oil, 1-3 phr Ca-Zn stabilizer, 0.1-0.5 phr processing aid; avoid fillers and pigments to preserve transparency
process_recipe.equipment_class: precision balance and dry blending mixer
inputs: Polymeric polyester plasticizer or trioctyl trimellitate

process_recipe.description: Premix dry blend until homogeneous and free-flowing
process_recipe.conditions: Room temperature to about 60 C; 10-20 min mixing; avoid moisture contamination

process_recipe.description: Melt compound the formulation to fully fuse PVC and disperse additives
process_recipe.conditions: Twin-screw microcompounder or internal mixer; melt temperature typically 160-185 C; residence time minimized to avoid degradation; inert venting desirable
process_recipe.equipment_class: lab twin-screw extruder or torque rheometer/internal mixer

process_recipe.description: Form test articles as film, sheet, tubing, or molded plaques
process_recipe.conditions: Compression molding at about 170-185 C or film extrusion/calandering; use polished tooling for optical clarity
process_recipe.equipment_class: compression press, film die, or small tubing extruder

process_recipe.description: Condition samples and evaluate transparency, flexibility, barrier, and chemical compatibility
process_recipe.conditions: Condition 24-48 h at ambient; test tensile properties, Shore hardness, oxygen or CO2 permeability, and immersion in representative acids, bases, oils, alcohols, acetone/MEK/THF/NMP/DMF, and salt solutions

process_recipe.description: Iterate formulation to reduce solvent extraction and improve barrier if needed
process_recipe.conditions: Decrease monomeric plasticizer content, increase polymeric plasticizer fraction, or slightly increase PVC resin fraction; reassess clarity-flexibility balance

manufacturing_process.feedback_to_system2: Manufacturable at lab scale with standard PVC compounding and film/molding equipment. However, broad resistance to aprotic solvents remains the principal performance risk versus THV. Validation must focus on solvent-specific swelling and plasticizer extraction. If the application includes frequent exposure to ketones, chlorinated solvents, THF, NMP, DMF, or similar aggressive aprotic media, flexible PVC may fail and the requirement set is likely overconstrained for a transparent PFAS-free flexible single-material substitute.
